results.title: The Best Dictation Software
results.content:
Wispr Flow shows a small indicator on the bottom of your computer’s screen to quickly launch dictation or create a voice snippet anytime.Wispr Flow

It works everywhere. Wispr Flow is like a walkie-talkie. You press and hold a user-configurable shortcut (by default, that’s Fn on macOS or Ctrl+Win on Windows) to start dictation, and then you release it when you’re finished speaking. Wispr Flow waits until you stop, and then it transcribes your speech into your selected application. It also offers a hands-free-mode shortcut for longer dictation sessions. [...] Dragon Professional Anywheredictation software has been on the market since 1990, and it was 95% accurate in our testing. It can add formatting in Office applications, it correctly recognizes punctuation commands, and it has a custom dictation vocabulary. But it works only on Windows and mobile devices, and it requires an internet connection. It does not support keyboard integration on mobile, and it’s worse at recognizing names and ordinal numbers than our picks. Also, it can be purchased only through an enterprise sales process, so it’s more difficult and annoying to get than other dictation tools. [...] Dictation pauses if you switch applications. Word’s Dictate continuously types what you say as long as the Word window is active in the foreground. If you switch applications or tabs in your browser, dictation stops.

You have to type in Microsoft 365 apps. Microsoft includes the Dictate feature in Word as well as in other Microsoft 365 applications, such as OneNote, Outlook, and PowerPoint. But you cannot use the Dictate feature to insert text directly into any other applications.

Instead, you’d have to first use Dictate to type text in Word, and then select and copy the text and paste it into your other application. That makes it a better choice only for typing long-form, formatted documents. Alternatively, Windows’ built-in dictation offers similar dictation features when online.

results.title: 10 Best Dictation Software Tools June 2026
results.content:
System-wide dictation that works across apps and the Windows desktop

Voice navigation commands for basic cursor control and text formatting

Good for: Windows users with occasional dictation needs who want a no-cost option.

Limitation: Accuracy relies on manual training that does not improve automatically; latency runs above 700ms and the interface is more basic than modern alternatives.

Bottom line: A workable starting point for light use on Windows, but accuracy plateaus without ongoing manual training.

### 6. Wispr Flow

Wispr Flow is a modern AI-powered dictation tool built for Mac and Windows. It works across apps and focuses on natural, conversational voice input for everyday professional workflows.

Key features:

AI-powered transcription that works across Mac and Windows apps [...] System-wide dictation that works across apps and the Windows desktop

Limitation: Accuracy relies on manual training that does not improve automatically; latency runs above 700ms and the interface is more basic than modern alternatives.

### 6. Wispr Flow

Wispr Flow is a modern AI-powered dictation tool built for Mac and Windows. It works across apps and focuses on natural, conversational voice input for everyday professional workflows.

Key features:

AI-powered transcription that works across Mac and Windows apps [...] Good for: Students and casual users who need basic transcription inside a browser at no cost.

Limitation: Browser-only with no system-wide functionality; accuracy and features fall behind paid alternatives.

Bottom line: A free, low-friction entry point for light use, but it cannot replace a full dictation app for professional work.

### 10. Voice In

Voice In is a Chrome extension that adds voice input to any text field inside the Chrome browser. It supports over 50 languages and includes custom voice commands that can be mapped to frequently used phrases or actions.

Key features:

Works in any Chrome text field across web apps without additional setup

Supports 50+ languages for multilingual users

Custom voice commands that map spoken phrases to text shortcuts

results.title: Dictation Software – Disability Resources and Educational Services
results.content:
### See it in action

Dragon offers demo videos that showcase its features and offer tips and tricks for better dictation. Their Dictation video gives a good overview of what NaturallySpeaking looks like in action.

## Free Dictation Alternatives

### Microsoft Dictate

Platform: Microsoft 365.  
How to get it: Dictate is part of Microsoft’s Immersive Reader tool suite, which supports Universal Design for Learning. It comes built into Word, OneNote, and Outlook.

### Google Docs Voice Typing

Platform: Chrome browser.  
How to get it: Voice Typing comes built into Google Docs in Chrome.

### Windows Speech

Platform: Windows.  
How to get it: Speech comes built into Windows.

### Apple Dictation

Platforms: Mac, iOS.  
How to get it: Dictation comes built into Mac and iOS. [...] + Note: While dictation software offers some hands-free functionality, there are other alternatives for fully controlling a computer without a mouse or keyboard.
 People who want to focus on getting their thoughts down without worrying about spelling
 People who find it easier or quicker to convey their thoughts aloud than through writing
 People who experience “blank page paralysis” from looking at an empty page [...] Below is an example of a dictation program used at the University of Illinois, as well as some free alternatives available to anyone. The free alternatives listed are by no means exhaustive, and many won’t have all the features available from paid programs. However, they do give a good example of the basic functionalities available, and may help determine whether it’s worth pursuing more feature-rich alternatives.

results.title: The Best Dictation Software of 2026 | Reviews by Wirecutter
results.content:
Dragon Professional Anywhere dictation software has been on the market since 1990, and it was 95% accurate in our testing. It can add formatting in Office applications, it correctly recognizes punctuation commands, and it has a custom dictation vocabulary. But it works only on Windows and mobile devices, and it requires an internet connection. It does not support keyboard integration on mobile, and it’s worse at recognizing names and ordinal numbers than our picks. Also, it can be purchased only through an enterprise sales process, so it’s more difficult and annoying to get than other dictation tools. [...] Instead, you’d have to first use Dictate to type text in Word, and then select and copy the text and paste it into your other application. That makes it a better choice only for typing long-form, formatted documents. Alternatively, Windows’ built-in dictation offers similar dictation features when online.

You can’t add custom words. You can add custom words to Word’s spelling dictionary, and since Dictate runs alongside Word’s built-in autocorrect, that might prevent it from rewriting a word it otherwise might type incorrectly. But there is no way to add words to Dictate itself or to train it on custom vocabulary.

## How to get the best results from dictation software [...] These include specific commands for opening and closing quotes, specific brackets or parentheses, apostrophe S, and hyphens or em and en dashes — as opposed to relying on cleanup or autocorrect tools to add the correct variants. Symbols — including copyright signs, paragraph marks, and a handful of currency signs — are also supported, as are a few text emojis.

Even more useful are formatting commands to align, indent, and format text as superscript or subscript; to add or remove bold, italics, and underlining; or to format text into a list. We were also able to add a heading by asking Word to “set Heading 1,” even though this command is not officially documented.

Microsoft Word’s Dictate feature works on every platform: web, desktop, and mobile. Microsoft Word

results.title: Best AI-Powered Speech Recognition & Dictation App for Mac? Looking for Quality & Features! : r/macapps
results.content:
I'd say if you're not a power user, or don't plan to use the AI aspect beyond basic transcription/formatting, you’ll likely be satisfied with MacWhisper (or many of the other alternatives that keep popping everywhere)—even SuperWhisper has a free plan that may be enough. For me SuperWhisper has replaced not only dictation apps, but also every other AI app subscription... I use it for basic coding, writing or summarizing articles, content generation, as an AI assistant, etc.

Both are great in terms of accuracy, but they both do some things better than the other. Also both offer one time payment options, though SW is considerable more expensive (though I still see it as a huge deal considering unlimited AI)
